categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion and Developer Activity - May 15, 2026

content.text: In the general discussion channel, community members exchanged greetings throughout the day. One member, IVgamer, noted excitement about a new partnership announcement, describing it as significant for the project's recognition and growth. A question was raised about an update related to Eliza OZ, to which enigma289 confirmed they were actively working on it. Odilitime responded with a question mark to IVgamer's partnership comment, suggesting some uncertainty or curiosity about the details. Overall the channel saw light social interaction with no major technical discussions.

In the coders channel, a developer named ReaWorks introduced themselves and described a project they are exploring: a simple escrow primitive for AI-agent work built on Base. The design involves a buyer locking funds, an agent or seller completing work, and the buyer releasing payment, with dispute and timeout paths kept minimal to ensure the incentive structure remains auditable. ReaWorks expressed interest in understanding how agent frameworks like elizaOS should interface with payment and settlement flows without embedding excessive trust logic into the agent runtime itself. This reflects a broader interest in integrating financial primitives with AI agent systems in a transparent and secure way.
No activity was recorded for the date 2026-05-15. There is no data available to summarize for this period.

In the general discussion channel, community members raised several topics. A user requested an unban for someone named arisae, to which a moderator responded that no one by that name appeared to be banned. Another user asked about the release of V3, noting that Shaw had previously indicated it would arrive soon, though no confirmed release date was provided. Separately, a discussion emerged around a reported partnership between the Secure Blockchain Development Corp and the Eliza Foundation, aimed at commercializing an Agentic SME solution using the ElizaOS framework. One user described it as a Canadian listed entity deal with a 30-day timeline for a v1 and approximately 90 days for full deployment. Another user expressed skepticism, suggesting nothing had been formally signed, which prompted a rebuttal defending the legitimacy and outlined timeline of the partnership. In the coders channel, activity was minimal with only a casual greeting posted.
No activity was recorded for the date of 2026-05-16. There is no data or events to report for this period.

A user named samuel1 raised concerns in the discussion channel about missing the migration deadline for ai16z to ElizaOS. They mentioned having sent an email but received no response, and asked for assistance from admins or community members to help with the migration process.

A full-stack developer named Keil introduced themselves in both the discussion and coders channels, highlighting experience with React, Node.js, Python, OpenAI API, and PyTorch. They specialize in web platforms, internal tools, automation systems, and AI-integrated applications, and expressed interest in collaborating on interesting projects while freelancing.

In the coders channel, a concept described as FIFA for AI Agents was shared, referring to a Football Fantasy league designed for AI Agents. The link was shared alongside an image previewing the concept.

In the partners channel, DannyNOR NoFapArc noted that a few AI-related coins are gaining traction, primarily on the Base blockchain. They expressed cautious optimism about a potential turnaround, noting that the outcome depends largely on the project's plans going forward.

Development on May 17, 2026 centered on hardening cross-platform infrastructure across three main areas: Live USB stability, Android local-inference, and CI/CD consolidation. On the OS infrastructure side, the team advanced the Live USB architecture with production-ready update manifests, systemd-based health checks, refined branded launcher labels, UDisks configurations, and validated root-mode boot configurations and live demo paths. For Android local-inference and voice features, the team stabilized native loader integration for stock Android APKs, enabled native TTS playback, improved voice hint forwarding for streaming plugins, and added long-generation support for Android direct paths to optimize chat performance. CI/CD improvements included resolving Docker build failures by consolidating workflows and merging image configuration files, as well as improving cloud reliability by integrating node health checks into the provisioning-worker daemon. Two issues require urgent team attention: the OpenVINO Runtime RFC needs input to finalize recommender logic and prevent accidental LLM routing, and the Speculative Decoding SWA Gate requires ongoing validation of a new fallback patch across 27B-class models. On the positive side, two issues were closed: M-RoPE position validation and speculative decoding compatibility on Lunar Lake/Vulkan were resolved, and an investigation into Qwen2.5-14B bandwidth performance concluded with no persistent kernel regression found. Active work in progress includes fixes for circular symlink issues in the app-core pruner, connector capability detection and sub-agent UX redesign, first-party satellites and dynamic view host bridge development, shell foundation improvements with accessibility and contrast enhancements, and a Swift runtime RPC bridge.

categories.title: Community Discussion and Developer Introductions in ElizaOS Discord

content.text: In the general discussion channel, community members exchanged greetings and several developers introduced themselves and their work. WizphicsX reached out seeking contact information for the leadership team handling strategic partnerships. A developer named Ginga introduced themselves as a builder of AI and full-stack systems, emphasizing practical, production-ready software focused on efficiency, accuracy, and security. Another user, _0rkz_, introduced BYTE Protocol, a per-byte data settlement layer built on Arbitrum where publishers stake tokens and broadcast data feeds while agents pay per kilobyte. The protocol enforces data quality on-chain, slashing bad data and rewarding good publishers with up to 70 percent take rate, with over 10,000 messages settled on testnet and 8 live feeds including BTC price and a fact-oracle backed by Claude web search. Keil also introduced themselves as a full-stack developer experienced with React, Node.js, Python, OpenAI API, and PyTorch, focusing on AI tools and workflow automation and open to collaboration. A bot alert also flagged a failed Pushover notification configuration in the channel.

In the coders channel, several developers posted introductions and sought technical connections. Keil reached out to Baoger about potential collaboration given a background in full-stack AI workflow engineering. Xpes asked the community for help connecting a Claude AI agent to AgentKey, describing it as a universal connection layer for AI agents to interact with external tools and APIs, noting they were new to this area and looking for setup guidance. Keil also posted a general introduction highlighting experience with React, Node.js, Python, OpenAI API, and PyTorch, and openness to freelance collaboration. Trace introduced themselves as an AI and full-stack engineer specializing in LLM systems, agents, RAG, multimodal AI, and automation, describing their strength as transitioning projects from fragile prototypes and scattered tools to reliable, scalable, production-ready AI systems, and noting availability for freelance or contract work. Harry38485 shared a project case study involving a complex web platform built with Next.js 15, React 19, TypeScript, Tailwind, Prisma, PostgreSQL, Redis, Docker, Kubernetes on AWS, with LangGraph and CrewAI agents powered by Claude 3.5 and GPT-4o, and advanced RAG over Pinecone and Weaviate, expressing interest in similar end-to-end full-stack and AI projects.

Development on May 18, 2026 centered on cross-platform stability and foundational infrastructure for deployment and benchmarking. On the stability front, Windows fallbacks were implemented for plugin-lifeops and circular symlink issues in the Docker pruner were resolved. CI/CD capabilities were expanded with experimental ARM64 support and Flatpak manifests prepared for offline-only build environments. For deployment infrastructure, the foundation for the elizaos deploy CLI was launched to streamline monetized app management. Additionally, a side-by-side comparison harness for SWE-bench Lite was integrated to enable performance evaluation against opencode.

categories.title: ElizaOS v2.0.0 Release and Community Discussion

content.text: ElizaOS v2.0.0 was published on May 19, 2026, announced in the discussion channel and met with positive reactions from community members. A community member noted that v3 is also expected to launch soon.

A community member raised several questions about elizaOS token economics and project structure. Core team member Odilitime provided clarifications: Milady and elizaOS are not the same legal entity, as the team took fees and built something on their own stack. Milady has its own cloud infrastructure, and profits from it would fuel Milady buybacks rather than elizaOS buybacks. No additional tokens beyond the existing ones are planned, with a possible Babylon token considered far in the future. There is currently no formalized buyback percentage, as the focus remains on achieving profitability first. No formal governance mechanism for buyback decisions has been established yet.

In the coders channel, a developer announced the recent publication of their SDK and expressed interest in integrating ElizaOS. Odilitime mentioned working on trading bots. Several developers also introduced themselves in both channels, highlighting backgrounds in full-stack development, AI systems, LLM orchestration, RAG, automation, and tools built with React, Node.js, Python, OpenAI API, and PyTorch, and expressed openness to freelance or collaborative opportunities.

categories.title: ElizaOS Community Discussion: Technical Milestones, Token Focus, and Developer Activity

content.text: A notable technical milestone was shared in the discussion channel where Shaw posted an image showcasing Eliza v3 running on ElizaOS (Linux) on an emulated RISC-V chip referred to as the Eliza E1. Community member Zaday celebrated this achievement, describing it as ElizaOS Debian RISC-V 64-bit ISO successfully booting on the QEMU chip emulator in 35 seconds. This sparked curiosity about whether the team intends to develop their own dedicated chip for ElizaOS in the future.

Regarding token strategy, a community member asked about a previously mentioned airdrop to ElizaOS holders, referencing either a Milady or Babylon related drop. Shaw clarified that the Babylon airdrop did not happen due to running out of funds, and confirmed the team is now fully focused on ElizaOS. Zaday expressed strong support for this direction, advocating that the core team concentrate solely on the ElizaOS token and build multiple utility use cases for it similar to ETH or SOL, leaving new token creation to the broader market. Separately, a question arose about which Milady token receives buybacks from Milady app revenue, with Odilitime indicating it is the BNB version.

In the coders channel, a full stack and AI developer introduced themselves and outlined their areas of interest including AI integrations, workflow automation, SaaS platforms, backend systems and APIs, AI agents and assistants, dashboards and analytics tools, and productivity and business-focused applications. They expressed interest in connecting with founders and developers working on long-term projects. Additionally, in the general discussion channel, a developer announced the release of an Eliza plugin for DeFi agents on Base and Aave, published to NPM under the package name centinel-risk-protocol/eliza-plugin-centinel, offering real-time solvency scoring and flash-crash simulations, and invited community members to test it and provide feedback.

categories.title: elizaOS Community Discussion and Development Updates

content.text: elizaOS v2.0 was recently released with a heavy focus on native apps, as confirmed by community moderator Odilitime. Several community members returning after time away or joining for the first time asked about the current state of the platform, how to use it, and whether video or live examples exist to demonstrate its capabilities. A new user also checked in looking to get started with elizaOS. An upcoming Agent Dev Talk Twitter Space was shared by Odilitime, hosted by odi and cozydev, providing a live audio venue for developers to discuss agents. Additionally, a community member shared a link referencing upcoming agent activity related to a Spaces event featuring Clawville World.

In the coders channel, a founder of a top VoiceAI white label agency platform discussed the challenges of dockerizing their platform for local deployment. They noted progress in reducing their system down to two containers and are now working toward a single container solution for easier mass deployment. A full stack and AI developer offered advice, highlighting that the hardest parts of local deployment are typically handling real-time voice dependencies, GPU access, networking, persistent storage, and maintaining low latency across different environments. They recommended separating services such as inference, API, database, and queue or worker, then containerizing them gradually rather than all at once. The same developer also introduced themselves to the community, sharing interest in AI integrations, workflow automation, SaaS platforms, backend systems, AI agents, dashboards, and productivity tools, and expressed openness to connecting with founders and developers on long-term projects.
